name: Longitudinal residential monitoring fidelity
consequences: Immediate: Sensor calibration and data capture protocols tighten to improve detection of infrequent parasomnia events. → Systemic: Event yield increases by 15% as measured by adjudicated episodes per participant, but false alarm rate rises by 5% requiring additional review capacity. → Strategic: Higher data quality accelerates phenotype characterization but increases staffing and operational costs, challenging the plan’s cost ceiling.
options: Maintain the current nightly monitoring cadence with incremental calibration of sensors to improve event detection without increasing scope.
Adopt a data-driven triage rule to escalate to enhanced PSG only on pre-defined triggers derived from sensor fusion and preliminary annotations.
Launch a cloud-enabled, modular full-night PSG platform on-demand with a data-as-a-service licensing model for external researchers.
review: Controls data fidelity vs. cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and regulatory review timelines required to scale data-sharing across platforms.

name: Data governance and access architecture
consequences: Immediate: Tiered data access and dynamic consent reduce privacy risk and streamline internal approvals. → Systemic: Time-to-data-use approvals decreases by 40%, but broader external collaboration options shrink by 25%, constraining rapid cross-center validation. → Strategic: Governance framework sustains ethical integrity while balancing openness with monetization potential.
options: Maintain existing de-identification and data-sharing constraints while ensuring compliance.
Implement tiered data access with dynamic consent and granular data-use agreements to balance openness and privacy.
Launch a federated analytics framework with secure enclaves and a paid research collaboration model that monetizes access to anonymized datasets while preserving privacy.
review: Controls privacy vs openness. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability challenges and governance overhead across multiple partner institutions.

name: Recruitment and enrollment optimization
consequences: Immediate: Structured pre-admission screening improves enrollment efficiency by reducing screen-fail rates. → Systemic: Enrollment yield rises by 12% but recruitment costs increase by 18% per participant, affecting cost efficiency. → Strategic: The unit attains higher sample diversity and statistical power at greater unit cost, requiring budget reallocation or fundraising.
options: Continue existing recruitment channels with modest regional expansion.
Introduce structured pre-admission screening clinics and formal referrals to improve yield.
Establish a tele-recruitment platform with performance-based incentives and predictive screening to accelerate enrollment.
review: Controls recruitment channels vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant burden and consent complexity across platforms.

name: Staffing, safety, and governance framework
consequences: Immediate: Rotating night coverage with cross-training reduces fatigue-related errors and improves response times. → Systemic: Staffing costs rise by 12% and coverage reliability improves by 8 percentage points, trading off budget pressure for safety and throughput. → Strategic: The improved safety and throughput justify higher personnel costs but necessitate ongoing funding and efficiency measures.
options: Maintain current staffing with minor SOP refinements.
Implement rotating night coverage with cross-training and standardized SOPs.
Adopt a flexible, on-demand staffing marketplace with performance-based payments and strict safety rails.
review: Controls staffing flexibility vs cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints on shift patterns and recruitment pipelines.

name: Technological platform and data infrastructure
consequences: Immediate: Modular sensor suite and synchronized data streams improve interoperability and reduce downtime. → Systemic: Data processing throughput increases by 30% with a 10% capex increase, trading speed for cost. → Strategic: Enables scalable, shareable analytics while requiring longer-term capital commitments.
options: Continue current modular sensor stack and data pipeline with incremental upgrades.
Enhance automated quality-control pipelines and real-time annotation prompts.
Create an open ecosystem data platform with containerized analysis pipelines and a data-licensing model for external researchers.
review: Controls throughput vs capital expenditure. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor lock-in risks and data standardization across partners.

name: Ethics, safety, and privacy controls
consequences: Immediate: Privacy-preserving measures reduce risk and facilitate consent processing. → Systemic: A privacy-by-design approach lowers breach probability by 40% but may constrain some data-sharing capabilities by 10-20%, trading openness for safety. → Strategic: Strengthens trust and regulatory alignment at potential cost to external collaborations.
options: Maintain current ethics approvals with routine audits.
Implement continuous risk monitoring and enhanced on-site safety drills.
Adopt privacy-by-design with differential privacy techniques, automated consent management, and a live data-sharing marketplace.
review: Controls privacy vs openness. Weakness: The options fail to consider ongoing governance for consent withdrawal and cross-border data transfers.

name: Naturalistic residence fidelity optimization
consequences: Immediate: The residence adopts a more naturalistic environment → Systemic: Data completeness improves from 78% to 86% and usable nights rise by about 1 per participant; however, renovation and ongoing costs increase by ~12% and staffing needs grow ~15% (trade-off: ecological validity vs budget and throughput) → Strategic: The study yields more ecologically valid phenotypes and stronger publication potential. Operational constraints require revised budgeting. This slows ramp-up but enhances external credibility for grant applications.
options: Enhance safety and comfort features while maintaining current surveillance intensity to protect privacy and minimize disruption
Introduce structured naturalistic observation modules with predefined escalation criteria to preserve data fidelity while controlling risk
Adopt edge-computing enabled, privacy-preserving on-device scoring with a data-cooperative governance model to share de-identified insights with external researchers
review: Controls naturalistic environment fidelity vs. participant privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

name: Tiered PSG escalation protocol
consequences: Immediate: Tiered PSG escalation protocol is implemented with predefined triggers → Systemic: Average nights per participant increase from 1.8 to 2.4 and escalation costs rise ~10%, while diagnostic yield improves by 6 percentage points (trade-off: thoroughness vs staffing burden) → Strategic: The protocol enhances data richness and result validity but requires greater budget and personnel planning. This may constrain enrollment pace if budgets are fixed. A cross-institution collaboration could mitigate costs.
options: Maintain nightly low-burden PSG monitoring with fixed escalation triggers to minimize disruption
Integrate automated flag-based escalations for probable events and reserve additional PSG nights for high-yield cases
Establish universal escalation via mobile PSG units and cloud-based central scoring with cross-institution data exchange
review: Controls escalation thoroughness vs. staffing costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider staffing constraints.

name: Semi-automated event triage workflow
consequences: Immediate: Semi-automated triage workflow is adopted with rule-based pre-filtering → Systemic: Time to adjudicate events decreases from 12 minutes to 8 minutes per event and inter-rater reliability improves from kappa 0.78 to 0.86, but false positives risk increases by ~1.5 percentage points (trade-off: throughput vs accuracy) → Strategic: The workflow elevates throughput and consistency while preserving human oversight. This requires ongoing QA and calibration.
options: Maintain dual human scoring with expanded guidelines to improve reliability
Implement rule-based triage pre-filtering to prioritize high-probability events for manual review
Deploy machine-augmented triage with real-time cross-scoring and secure external validation via a data portal
review: Controls triage automation efficiency vs. human review accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider false-positive rate and missed events.

name: Participant retention and engagement scaffolding
consequences: Immediate: Participant engagement scaffolding is introduced to maintain enthusiasm → Systemic: Enrollment conversion rises from 40% to 58% and dropout rates drop by 15%, but staff time for engagement increases ~20% (trade-off: participant burden vs data completeness) → Strategic: Improved retention yields richer longitudinal data and more robust analyses. Operational implications include staffing reallocation and schedule flexibility. This strategy strengthens study power but raises ongoing labor costs.
options: Maintain current compensation and scheduling to minimize burden
Introduce tiered engagement with flexible visit windows, caregiver involvement, and feedback loops
Co-create participant governance with dashboards, optional extended on-site lodging, and involvement in study design
review: Controls participant engagement vs staff burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider caregiver capacity.

name: Cross-site data harmonization and EEG-BIDS standardization
consequences: Immediate: Cross-site harmonization initiatives begin → Systemic: Data latency reduces from ~28 days to ~14 days and interoperability improves by ~40%, but governance overhead increases (trade-off: interoperability vs governance overhead) → Strategic: Enables robust multi-site phenotyping and external collaboration. This will require formal data-use agreements and ongoing coordination across sites.
options: Implement local data schemas aligned with Bonn protocols without cross-site changes
Establish EEG-BIDS compatibility and shared metadata templates across Bonn and partner centers
Create a cross-institution data-sharing consortium with standardized pipelines, secure de-identification, and open-access de-identified datasets via a research portal
review: Controls cross-site harmonization vs. data latency. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability across legacy systems.

name: Privacy-preserving video handling and de-identification pipeline
consequences: Immediate: Privacy-preserving controls for video streams are strengthened → Systemic: Access requests processing time reduces from 7 days to 3 days and de-identification success rate increases to 99.5%; however processing costs rise ~6% (trade-off: privacy vs data utility) → Strategic: Maintains participant trust while enabling broader data use and collaboration. This requires robust edge processing and governance for external access.
options: Maintain strict on-storage de-identification and role-based access controls
Implement automated anonymization with audit trails and consent-driven access revocation
Develop edge-processing video analytics and secure multi-party computation to enable external collaboration without exposing raw footage
review: Controls video privacy vs data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider feasibility of edge processing.

name: Parasomnia capture protocol optimization
consequences: Immediate: Standardizing nightly low-burden monitoring increases the probability of capturing at least one parasomnia event per participant on a typical stay by 15%. Systemic: Event yield rises by 25%, but annotation workload increases by 12% and average stay duration extends by 0.5–1 day, trading data richness for higher staffing and cost. Strategic: Improves longitudinal phenotyping power and generalizability, but requires budget reallocation and enhanced governance for data volume.
options: Standardize nightly low-burden monitoring across all participants to maximize data comparability.
Incorporate adaptive sampling with selective enhanced nights based on interim yield to balance data richness with participant burden.
Launch federated, privacy-preserving edge-processing with cross-site data-sharing consortium to scale phenotyping without central raw data transfer.
review: Controls data capture depth vs. staffing burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider cost escalation and scheduling bottlenecks.

name: Cross-site privacy-preserving data ecosystem design
consequences: Immediate: Enables cross-site analyses without transferring identifiable data. Systemic: Decreases data transfer by 70% and increases collaboration breadth by 60%, but adds governance overhead and consent management costs of 25%. Strategic: Broadens external validity and funding potential, but slows decision cycles due to governance processes.
options: Limit data sharing to de-identified aggregated statistics with local processing.
Establish standardized metadata and secure transfer protocols to enable controlled cross-site analyses.
Create a data-services platform funded by a multi-stakeholder data-commons model, offering tiered access to de-identified datasets and on-demand analytics.
review: Controls data access vs. privacy and safety compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory alignment and dynamic consent overhead.

name: Participant engagement economics
consequences: Immediate: Incentives boost enrollment by 20% over six months. Systemic: Per-participant cost declines 10% due to improved retention, but total administrative overhead rises 15%. Strategic: Accelerates enrollment velocity and supports broader recruitment, but requires robust grant management and governance oversight.
options: Maintain existing per-night compensation and meals with fixed schedule.
Introduce tiered incentives and flexible visit timing to boost enrollment while monitoring budget.
Adopt pay-for-performance model with philanthropic micro-grants to accelerate recruitment and offer co-authorship opportunities.
review: Controls incentives vs. budget stability. Weakness: The options fail to consider donor dependency risk and long-term sustainability.

name: Phased modular facility expansion
consequences: Immediate: Expansion readiness reduces downtime during reconfiguration by 2 weeks. Systemic: Capacity increases to 12 suites; occupancy utilization improves by 25%, but upfront capex up 40% and financing complexity. Strategic: Enables scalable expansion and cross-site replication, but raises governance complexity and project risk.
options: Maintain 8-suite footprint with safety upgrades and no structural changes.
Phase in 12-suite capacity using modular walls and gating, contingent on data quality.
Deploy fully modular suite pods with telepresence-enabled operations and remote facility management.
review: Controls expansion speed vs. governance complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long lead times for regulatory approvals and safety certifications.

name: Dynamic consent and ethics governance
consequences: Immediate: Participants can adjust consent preferences in real time. Systemic: Compliance agility improves by 40%, but requires new software and governance overhead rising 20%. Strategic: Improves trust and flexibility for adaptive protocols, but increases ongoing operational complexity.
options: Maintain current consent processes and annual ethics reviews.
Implement periodic re-consent checkpoints and privacy risk dashboards.
Implement dynamic consent platform with real-time preference controls and a cross-institution ethics charter.
review: Controls consent flexibility vs. administrative overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider technological maintenance burden and user-interface usability.

name: Advanced event triage data-in-motion
consequences: Immediate: Triage speed improves by 25%, reducing manual review time. Systemic: Throughput improves 30%, but sync overhead and training require 15% more staff time. Strategic: Accelerates publication and method validation while increasing IT governance needs.
options: Maintain dual human scoring with standard workflow.
Introduce semi-automated triage with probabilistic tagging to prioritize reviews.
Deploy privacy-preserving cross-site federated triage engine with edge processing and clinician-in-the-loop validation.
review: Controls annotation throughput vs. cross-site data privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider integration with existing scoring criteria and data-label drift.
